_meta.description: Education mode modules for Great Vespers — designed for inquirers from American protestant/evangelical backgrounds
layers: title — display heading for the module
historicalSnapshot — timeline entries with era, date, and detail
scripture — key Bible references with labels
sources — scholarly, popular, and early church references

modules.title: The Creation Psalm
modules.subtitle: Beginning at the beginning
content: Vespers opens with Psalm 103 (104 in Western numbering) — a sweeping hymn of creation that moves from light to water to mountains to animals to human labor to the sea. It is not read quietly; it is sung, often with great solemnity, as the priest censes the entire church. The incense rises while the psalm recounts the world God made. It is the Church's way of starting at the beginning — literally. Every evening, worship opens with Genesis.

This is not arbitrary. In Jewish reckoning, the day begins at sunset — 'and there was evening and there was morning, the first day.' Vespers is the first service of the liturgical day, and so it begins where the Bible begins: with creation. The psalm is a poetic retelling of Genesis 1, verse by verse — light, the firmament, the waters, vegetation, the sun and moon, living creatures, and finally the breath of God that sustains it all. To pray this psalm at evening is to stand at the threshold of a new day and declare that the world belongs to God.

The censing that accompanies the psalm has its own deep roots. In the Jerusalem Temple, incense was offered twice daily — morning and evening — on the golden altar that stood before the Holy of Holies. Psalm 141 makes the connection explicit: 'Let my prayer arise in Thy sight as incense.' When the priest censes the church during Psalm 103, he is doing what Aaron and his sons did in the Tabernacle: sanctifying the space with fragrant smoke as a sign that the prayers of God's people are rising to His presence. The book of Revelation confirms the image: the angel stands at the altar with a golden censer, and the smoke of the incense rises with the prayers of the saints.

For visitors accustomed to worship that begins with a greeting or a praise set, this opening can feel surprising. There is no welcome, no announcements, no warm-up. The service begins with God — who He is and what He has made. Everything else follows from that.

historicalSnapshot.detail: Incense was offered morning and evening on the golden altar in the Temple (Exodus 30:7-8). The Levites sang psalms during the evening sacrifice. Psalm 104 was associated with the Sabbath eve.

The Ma'ariv (evening prayer service) opened with a blessing praising God who 'rolls away light before darkness and darkness before light' — creation language framing the evening, just as Psalm 103/104 does at Vespers.

The pilgrim Egeria describes the evening service in Jerusalem: at the lighting of the lamps, psalms are sung and incense is offered. The bishop enters and prays while the whole church is filled with the fragrance.

The Apostolic Constitutions prescribe evening psalm singing and prayer, with specific psalms appointed. The evening office was a universal practice across all major Christian centers by the fourth century.

scripture.label: "And there was evening and there was morning" — the day begins at sunset
"Bless the Lord, O my soul... who coverest Thyself with light as with a garment"
"In wisdom hast Thou made them all; the earth is full of Thy creatures"
Aaron burns incense morning and evening — the Temple pattern
"Let my prayer arise in Thy sight as incense"
Incense rising with the prayers of the saints before God's throne

scholarly: Robert Taft, 'The Liturgy of the Hours in East and West' (1986)
Paul Bradshaw, 'Daily Prayer in the Early Church' (1981)

modules.title: Blessed Is the Man
modules.subtitle: The whole Book of Psalms, read every week
content: After the opening psalm and the Great Litany, the choir sings the first section of Kathisma 1 — which is Psalm 1: 'Blessed is the man who walks not in the counsel of the ungodly.' The word kathisma means 'sitting' — during much of the service the congregation stands, but during the kathisma reading they may sit. It is a remnant of the ancient practice of reading large portions of the Psalter at every service.

The Orthodox Church reads through the entire Book of Psalms — all 150 psalms — every single week. During Great Lent, the cycle doubles: the full Psalter is read twice each week. The Psalter is divided into twenty kathismata (sections), and they are distributed across the daily services — Vespers, Matins, and the Hours — so that by Saturday evening, the cycle is complete and begins again. This is not a modern devotional plan; it is the continuation of a practice that predates Christianity.

In the synagogue, the Torah was read on a fixed annual cycle — the entire Pentateuch completed each year. The Psalter was read on a similar cycle, with specific psalms assigned to specific days and occasions. The early Christians inherited this and intensified it. The desert monastics of Egypt and Palestine in the fourth century recited the entire Psalter daily. The cathedral tradition (which Vespers follows) selected specific psalms for their thematic resonance: Psalm 1 at the beginning, because it sets out the two ways — the way of the righteous and the way of the ungodly.

On Saturday evenings at Great Vespers, 'Blessed Is the Man' is sung with particular solemnity — with alleluias between the verses and a melody that marks the transition from the working week into the Lord's Day. The psalm that opens the entire Psalter opens the most important Vespers of the week.

historicalSnapshot.detail: The Levites sang specific psalms at the daily Temple sacrifices. The Mishnah records which psalm was sung on which day of the week (Tamid 7:4). The full Psalter was used in rotating cycles.
Psalm 1 was understood as the gateway to the entire Psalter — a preface that frames all 150 psalms as instruction in the two ways. The Talmud treats Psalms 1 and 2 as a single opening unit.

The Desert Fathers recited the entire Psalter daily. Abba Apollo's community recited psalms continuously in shifts. John Cassian records the Egyptian practice of twelve psalms at evening and twelve at the night vigil.

The cathedral tradition appointed specific psalms for their thematic relevance: Psalm 103 for creation at evening, Psalm 1 as the gateway psalm. The twenty-kathisma system was standardized in the Typikon.

scripture.label: "Blessed is the man... his delight is in the law of the Lord"
"The Lord knows the way of the righteous, but the way of the ungodly shall perish"
"This Book of the Law shall not depart from your mouth; you shall meditate on it day and night"
Jesus refers to 'the Psalms' as one of the three divisions of Scripture
"Let the word of Christ dwell in you richly... singing psalms"
"Addressing one another in psalms and hymns and spiritual songs"

scholarly: Robert Taft, 'The Liturgy of the Hours in East and West' (1986)

modules.title: The Evening Sacrifice
modules.subtitle: Incense, psalms, and the hymns that change every day
content: The choir begins to sing: 'Lord, I have cried unto Thee, hear me. Hear me, O Lord. Lord, I have cried unto Thee, hear me; attend to the voice of my prayer when I cry unto Thee. Hear me, O Lord. Let my prayer arise in Thy sight as incense, and the lifting up of my hands as an evening sacrifice. Hear me, O Lord.' These verses from Psalms 140 and 141 have been sung at the evening service since the earliest centuries of Christianity.

The connection to Temple worship is direct. In the Jerusalem Temple, the evening sacrifice (the tamid) was offered every day at the ninth hour — around 3 PM. It included a lamb, a grain offering, and incense on the golden altar. Psalm 141 explicitly references this: 'Let my prayer arise as incense, and the lifting up of my hands as the evening sacrifice.' The early Christians, many of whom had worshiped in the Temple, understood their evening prayer as the spiritual continuation of the daily sacrifice — the offering of prayer instead of animals, as the prophets had foretold.

Between the psalm verses, the choir sings stichera — short hymns that change every day. This is where the content of the day's commemoration enters the service. On a Saturday, you will hear resurrectional hymns from the Octoechos (the eight-tone cycle), hymns for the saint of the day from the Menaion (the monthly book), and sometimes hymns from the Triodion during Lent. The last hymn before the Entrance is always a theotokion — a hymn to the Virgin Mary — and on Saturday evenings this is the dogmatikon, a hymn that expresses the doctrine of the Incarnation through the lens of Mary's role. These hymns are not decorative; they are the Church's theology set to music.

The pattern of psalm verses alternating with composed hymns goes back to the earliest Christian worship. The psalms are the scaffolding; the stichera are the Church's response to the psalms, connecting the ancient words to the feast or saint being celebrated. Every day of the year, the stichera change — which means the Church never repeats the same Vespers twice.

historicalSnapshot.detail: The evening tamid sacrifice — a lamb, grain offering, and incense — was offered daily at the ninth hour (3 PM). This was the sacrifice referenced in Psalm 141:2 and Daniel 9:21.

Peter and John went up to the Temple 'at the hour of prayer, the ninth hour' (Acts 3:1) — the time of the evening sacrifice. The first Christians continued attending Temple worship at its appointed hours.

Tertullian mentions that Christians pray at the ninth hour, connecting it to Peter and John's practice. Cyprian (~250 AD) explicitly links the ninth-hour prayer to the evening sacrifice of the Temple.
Egeria describes the evening service (lucernarium): psalms and antiphons are sung, the bishop enters, and Psalm 140 ('Let my prayer arise as incense') is sung — exactly as it is at Vespers today.

scripture.label: "Let my prayer arise in Thy sight as incense, and the lifting up of my hands as an evening sacrifice"
"Lord, I have cried unto Thee, hear me" — the opening of the section
Gabriel comes to Daniel 'at the time of the evening sacrifice'
Peter and John go to the Temple at the hour of prayer — the ninth hour
The daily evening sacrifice commanded — a lamb, grain, and wine
"In every place incense shall be offered to My name" — the prophetic vision
"We will offer the fruit of our lips" — prayer replacing animal sacrifice

scholarly: Robert Taft, 'The Liturgy of the Hours in East and West' (1986)
Paul Bradshaw, 'The Search for the Origins of Christian Worship' (2002)

modules.title: Light Entering Darkness
modules.subtitle: The ancient lamp-lighting ceremony
content: As evening falls, the deacon carries a censer through the Holy Doors, and the priest follows. This is the Entrance — one of the most ancient actions in Christian worship. In the early church, this moment was literal: as the sun set and the church grew dark, lamps were brought in, and the congregation greeted the light with prayer and song. The evening service was called the lucernarium — the lamp-lighting — and it was the heart of Vespers.

The symbolism is not subtle, and it is not invented. When the Tabernacle was built in the wilderness, God commanded that the lamps be tended every evening: 'Aaron shall burn incense on it, a perpetual incense before the Lord throughout your generations.' The evening lighting of the Temple lamps was one of the most sacred daily rituals. The menorah — the seven-branched lampstand — was to burn continually before the Lord. When the priest enters at Vespers, he is continuing this pattern: light and incense entering the holy place at evening.

The Christian meaning goes deeper still. John's Gospel opens with the declaration that Christ is 'the light that shines in the darkness, and the darkness has not overcome it.' The Entrance at Vespers makes this visible: as the natural light fades, the light of Christ enters the assembly. It is a daily reenactment of the hope that anchors all Christian worship — that darkness does not have the last word.

In some parishes, especially during Lent, the church is genuinely dark at this point in the service, and the effect is striking. The hymn that follows the Entrance — 'O Gladsome Light' — is one of the oldest Christian hymns in existence, and it was composed precisely for this moment: the greeting of the light at evening.

historicalSnapshot.detail: The evening lighting of the menorah and the burning of incense were commanded as a perpetual ordinance (Exodus 30:8, Leviticus 24:3). The priest entered the Holy Place each evening to tend the lamps.
The Sabbath began with the lighting of lamps at sunset. The Hanukkah festival celebrated the miraculous reigniting of the Temple menorah. Light at evening was deeply symbolic in Jewish worship.

Egeria describes the lucernarium in Jerusalem: as evening comes, the lamps are lit in the Anastasis (Church of the Resurrection), a deacon brings fire from within the tomb, and the bishop enters to pray.

Basil the Great writes: 'It seemed fitting to our fathers not to receive the gift of the evening light in silence, but to give thanks as soon as it appeared.' He identifies 'O Gladsome Light' as already ancient in his time.

scripture.label: Aaron burns incense and tends the lamps at evening — the pattern for the Entrance
"A lamp shall burn continually before the Lord"
"The light shines in the darkness, and the darkness has not overcome it"
"I am the light of the world" — Christ as the evening light
"O send out Thy light and Thy truth; let them lead me"
"The Lamb is its lamp" — the city that needs no sun

scholarly: Robert Taft, 'The Liturgy of the Hours in East and West' (1986)
Paul Bradshaw, 'Daily Prayer in the Early Church' (1981)
earlyChurch: Basil the Great, On the Holy Spirit 29 (~375 AD)

modules.title: The Oldest Christian Hymn
modules.subtitle: Sung every evening for almost two thousand years
content: Immediately after the Entrance, the congregation sings: 'O Gladsome Light of the holy glory of the immortal Father: heavenly, holy, blessed — Jesus Christ. Now that we have come to the setting of the sun and behold the light of evening, we praise God: Father, Son, and Holy Spirit.' This hymn — called Phos Hilaron in Greek — is the oldest known Christian hymn still in regular liturgical use. Its continuous history stretches back at least to the third century, and likely earlier.

Basil the Great, writing around 375 AD, already called it ancient. He attributed the tradition of singing a hymn at the lighting of the evening lamps to the practice of the earliest Christians, and noted that no one could remember who composed the Phos Hilaron — it was that old. Some scholars trace it to the second century. It may well be the oldest piece of Christian poetry that has never fallen out of use.

The hymn does something remarkable: it addresses Jesus Christ directly as 'light.' This is not metaphor — it is theology. The Nicene Creed calls Christ 'Light of Light, True God of True God,' and the Phos Hilaron expresses the same confession in the form of a song. At the exact moment when natural light fades, the Church turns to Christ and calls Him 'the light of evening.' The physical experience of sunset becomes a confession of faith.

For visitors from traditions where hymns are chosen by the worship leader each week, the idea of singing the same hymn every single evening can seem repetitive. But the effect is the opposite of monotony. The Phos Hilaron becomes like the Lord's Prayer — a text so familiar that it sinks into the bones, prayed not from the page but from the heart. It is the Church's evening breath.

historicalSnapshot.detail: The Phos Hilaron is composed — likely in the Greek-speaking Christian community. It is a Trinitarian hymn addressed to Christ as the 'gladsome light' of the Father. Its author is unknown.

Basil the Great writes that the hymn at the lighting of the lamps is already an ancient tradition: 'Who was the author of the hymn at the lighting of the lamps, Phos Hilaron? The people of old did not think it wrong to voice this hymn.'
Egeria describes the lighting of lamps and the singing of hymns and psalms at the evening service in Jerusalem — the lucernarium that the Phos Hilaron accompanies.
The earliest surviving manuscript of the Phos Hilaron is in the Apostolic Constitutions (~380 AD). The hymn was also found scratched into the walls of a Christian catacomb, suggesting popular use.

scripture.label: "In Him was life, and the life was the light of men"
"I am the light of the world"
"God who said 'Let light shine out of darkness' has shone in our hearts"
God "dwells in unapproachable light"
"The Father of lights, with whom there is no variation or shadow due to change"
"The Lord God will be their light" — the final vision of eternal evening light

scholarly: Robert Taft, 'The Liturgy of the Hours in East and West' (1986)
M. Kiley, 'Hymns of the Christian Church' (2003)
earlyChurch: Basil the Great, On the Holy Spirit 29 (~375 AD)

modules.title: The Psalm Refrain
modules.subtitle: A single verse, repeated — and why that matters
content: After the Gladsome Light, a single psalm verse is sung as a refrain — the prokeimenon. The word means 'that which is set before,' because it comes before the Scripture readings (when there are readings) or stands on its own as a kind of distilled psalm meditation. The deacon calls 'Let us attend,' the priest says 'Peace be unto all,' and then a cantor sings one verse, which the congregation repeats. A second verse follows, and the refrain is sung again.

This pattern — a verse sung, then repeated by the people — is pure synagogue practice. The Hallel psalms (113-118) were sung at the great Jewish festivals with exactly this structure: a leader chanting a verse and the congregation responding with a refrain. At the Passover Seder, Psalm 136 ('His mercy endures forever') was sung as a responsive refrain twenty-six times. The prokeimenon preserves this ancient structure in miniature: one verse, one refrain, sung and responded to.

Each day of the week has its own appointed prokeimenon. On Saturday evening, the prokeimenon is 'The Lord is King; He is robed in majesty' (Psalm 92:1) — a proclamation of God's sovereignty as the week ends. The prokeimena are not chosen at random; they were selected centuries ago for their thematic connection to the day. Saturday evening looks forward to the Resurrection. The other weekdays carry their own themes: creation, the cross, the saints, the departed.

On feasts and during special seasons, the prokeimenon changes. The Great Prokeimenon — a longer and more solemn version — appears on a few occasions each year. During Great Lent, pairs of prokeimena replace the ordinary ones, drawn from the Triodion. The system is intricate, but the principle is simple: a single psalm verse, chosen to capture the meaning of the day, proclaimed aloud and affirmed by the people.

historicalSnapshot.detail: The Levites sang psalm refrains during the daily sacrifices. The Hallel psalms were sung responsively at the great festivals — a leader chanting, the people responding.
The Passover Seder included Psalm 136 sung as a responsive refrain. The synagogue developed a system of psalm verses paired with Torah readings — the ancestor of the prokeimenon system.

Egeria describes the evening service in Jerusalem: after the hymns and psalms, specific verses were sung responsively by a cantor and the people — the earliest description of the prokeimenon structure.
The Typikon formalized the weekday prokeimenon cycle — one appointed verse per day, with special prokeimena for feasts and seasons.

scripture.label: Saturday evening prokeimenon: "The Lord is King; He is robed in majesty"
"His mercy endures forever" — the responsive refrain model, 26 times in one psalm
The Hallel refrain — "Let Israel now say: His mercy endures forever"
"They read from the Book of the Law, clearly, and gave the sense"
"I will sing with my spirit... how will the outsider say 'Amen'?"

scholarly: Robert Taft, 'The Liturgy of the Hours in East and West' (1986)
James McKinnon, 'The Temple, the Church Fathers, and Early Western Chant' (1998)

modules.title: Hymns Stitched to Verses
modules.subtitle: The psalms as a framework for the Church's poetry
content: Near the end of Vespers, the choir sings a set of hymns called the aposticha — from the Greek word meaning 'of the verses,' because each hymn is paired with a specific psalm verse. The pattern is: a psalm verse is chanted, then a hymn is sung in response. Verse, hymn. Verse, hymn. The psalm provides the scaffolding; the hymn provides the day's meditation.

This structure is not unique to the aposticha. It is the same pattern used earlier in 'Lord, I Have Cried,' and it appears throughout Matins and the other services as well. The technical term is 'sticheron' — a hymn 'stitched' to a verse. The entire Orthodox hymnographic tradition is built on this principle: the psalms are never left alone, but are always accompanied by the Church's interpretation. The Bible provides the text; the hymnographers provide the commentary.

On Saturday evenings, the aposticha come from the Octoechos — the eight-tone cycle — and the verses are drawn from psalms about the Resurrection: 'The Lord is King; He has robed Himself in majesty' and 'He has made the world firm; it shall never be moved.' On weekdays during Lent, the aposticha come from the Triodion, with verses about repentance and the departed. On feast days, the hymns celebrate the event or saint being commemorated. The last hymn is typically a theotokion — a hymn to the Virgin Mary — sung after 'Both now and ever.'

For visitors, the aposticha can be the most beautiful and theologically rich part of Vespers. These hymns were composed by some of the greatest poets of the Byzantine world — John of Damascus, Cosmas of Maiuma, Joseph the Hymnographer — and they compress entire theological reflections into a few lines of singable poetry. Each one is a miniature sermon.

historicalSnapshot.detail: The Levitical singers sang psalms with specific verses highlighted by antiphonal chanting. The pairing of verse-and-response is the oldest known form of liturgical music.
The earliest Christian hymnographers began composing troparia and stichera — short hymns keyed to psalm verses. Ephrem the Syrian (~306-373) pioneered the form in the Syriac tradition.

The great hymnographers — Andrew of Crete, John of Damascus, Cosmas of Maiuma, Joseph the Hymnographer — composed thousands of stichera that remain in use today, forming the backbone of the Octoechos and Menaion.

The Stoudite Typikon organized the hymn repertoire into the system still used: Octoechos for the weekly cycle, Menaion for fixed feasts, Triodion for Lent, Pentecostarion for Pascha through Pentecost.

scripture.label: "The Lord is King; He has robed Himself in majesty" — Saturday aposticha verse
"He has made the world firm; it shall never be moved" — second verse
"Teaching and admonishing one another in psalms and hymns and spiritual songs"
"Making melody to the Lord with your heart"
"O sing to the Lord a new song" — the call to compose hymns, not just repeat psalms

scholarly: Alexander Lingas, 'From Earth to Heaven: The Theology of Byzantine Chant' (2003)
Egon Wellesz, 'A History of Byzantine Music and Hymnography' (1961)

modules.title: Simeon's Farewell
modules.subtitle: The song of a man who had seen enough
content: As Vespers draws toward its close, the reader chants the prayer of Simeon from the Gospel of Luke: 'Lord, now lettest Thou Thy servant depart in peace, according to Thy word; for mine eyes have seen Thy salvation, which Thou hast prepared in the sight of all people: a light to enlighten the Gentiles and the glory of Thy people Israel.' It is one of the most personal and intimate texts in the entire service.

The story is in Luke 2. Simeon was an old man in Jerusalem who had been told by the Holy Spirit that he would not die until he had seen the Messiah. When Mary and Joseph brought the infant Jesus to the Temple for the purification rites commanded in the Law of Moses, Simeon took the child in his arms and spoke these words. He was saying: I have seen what I was waiting for. I can go now. The Church places this prayer at the end of Vespers — the end of the day — because every evening is a small departure, and every departure is made peaceful by the sight of salvation.

The prayer is called the Nunc Dimittis — Latin for 'now lettest Thou depart' — and it has been sung at the evening service in both Eastern and Western Christianity since the earliest centuries. Its presence at Vespers connects the close of each day to the close of a life: both are entrusted to God. Simeon's peace did not come from having accomplished everything, or from having answers to all his questions. It came from having seen the Christ, held Him, and known that the promise was real.

It is worth noting what Simeon also said, in the verses the service does not include: 'This child is destined for the falling and rising of many in Israel, and to be a sign that will be opposed — and a sword will pierce your own soul too.' The Nunc Dimittis is a prayer of peace, but not of naivety. Simeon knew what was coming. His peace was anchored in something stronger than circumstances.

historicalSnapshot.detail: Simeon, a righteous man 'waiting for the consolation of Israel,' takes the infant Jesus in his arms in the Temple and speaks the words that will become the Nunc Dimittis (Luke 2:25-35).
The Apostolic Constitutions prescribe the Nunc Dimittis for the evening service. It appears in multiple early liturgical manuscripts as a fixed element of Vespers and Compline.

Egeria's account of worship in Jerusalem includes evening prayers that conclude with readings and hymns from Luke's infancy narrative — the liturgical setting where the Nunc Dimittis naturally belongs.
The Nunc Dimittis is sung at Vespers in the Byzantine tradition and at Compline (Night Prayer) in the Western tradition — both placing Simeon's words at the close of the day.

scripture.label: Simeon's song — the full text of the Nunc Dimittis
Simeon's prophecy to Mary: "a sword will pierce your own soul"
"A light to the nations" — the prophecy Simeon is echoing
"I have given you as a covenant to the people, a light to the nations"
Jacob: "Now let me die, since I have seen your face" — the same pattern of seeing and departing in peace
Paul: "To depart and be with Christ... is far better" — the same peaceful readiness

scholarly: Raymond Brown, 'The Birth of the Messiah' (1993)
Robert Taft, 'The Liturgy of the Hours in East and West' (1986)

crossCuttingThemes.note: These themes appear across multiple modules and help bridge the gap between evangelical vocabulary and Orthodox practice

themes.bridge: Many evangelical churches have midweek services or small groups. Vespers is the ancient version — daily corporate prayer at the close of the day. The early church did not distinguish between 'Sunday worship' and 'everything else.' Prayer at set hours was the baseline.

Orthodox fixed prayers and evangelical spontaneous prayers are not opposites. Jesus prayed both ways — the Lord's Prayer is fixed; His prayer in Gethsemane is spontaneous. The Psalms are fixed prayers. The question is not 'fixed or free' but 'do you mean it?' Fixed prayers have one advantage: they teach you to pray things you would not think to pray on your own.

Daniel prayed three times a day facing Jerusalem. David sang psalms at morning and evening. The early church prayed at specific hours. The Orthodox daily cycle continues this — not as legalism, but as the discipline that keeps prayer from being crowded out by everything else in life.

The Psalter is the prayer book that Jesus used. He quoted psalms on the cross. The early church sang psalms in their gatherings. If the Book of Psalms is in your Bible, the question is not why Vespers uses so many psalms — it is why other services use so few.

books: Alexander Schmemann, 'Introduction to Liturgical Theology' (1966) — the single best overview of why the services are structured the way they are
Archimandrite Robert Taft, 'The Liturgy of the Hours in East and West' (1986) — the definitive scholarly history
Frederica Mathewes-Green, 'Welcome to the Orthodox Church' (2015) — accessible, written for visitors
podcasts: Ancient Faith Radio: ancientfaith.com — extensive library of talks on Orthodox worship
Lord of Spirits — explores the Old Testament roots of Christian worship
websites: OCA: oca.org/orthodoxy/the-orthodox-faith/worship — official overview of the daily services
